title: The IMF, World Bank & global policy
summary:
## Managing the global economy

International institutions coordinate policy, lend to countries in difficulty, and support development. The two most important — created at **Bretton Woods (1944)** — are the **IMF** and the **World Bank**, alongside the **WTO**.

## The International Monetary Fund (IMF)

**Role:** to promote global **monetary and financial stability**.
- Oversees the international monetary system and exchange rates.
- Provides **short-term loans** to members with **balance-of-payments/financial crises** (a lender of last resort for countries).
- Offers policy advice and surveillance.
**Conditionality:** IMF loans usually require **structural adjustment / austerity** — cutting deficits, reducing subsidies, devaluing/floating the currency, liberalising and privatising.
*Criticisms:* austerity can deepen recessions and hit the poor; a ‘one-size-fits-all’ approach; loss of economic **sovereignty**; moral hazard.

## The World Bank

**Role:** to reduce poverty and promote **long-term development**.
- Provides **long-term loans and grants** for development projects (infrastructure, education, health).
- Focuses on capacity building and structural reform in developing economies.
*Criticisms:* project failures, debt burdens, and past emphasis on conditions similar to the IMF's.

## The World Trade Organisation (WTO)

Promotes **free, rules-based trade**, negotiates trade liberalisation and settles disputes. Criticised for favouring developed economies and for slow progress (e.g. the Doha round).

## Structural adjustment and development debate

**Structural adjustment programmes (SAPs)** attach market-liberalising conditions to loans. Supporters say they restore stability and efficiency; critics say austerity, rapid liberalisation and subsidy cuts can raise poverty and unemployment in the short run. Modern approaches stress **institutions, governance and targeted poverty reduction (e.g. the SDGs)**.

## Egypt application

Egypt has run several **IMF-supported programmes** involving **EGP devaluation/flotation, subsidy reform and fiscal consolidation**. These aimed to restore macro stability and investor confidence but raised **inflation and living-cost pressures** in the short run — a textbook illustration of IMF conditionality trade-offs and the exchange-rate topics from Unit 9.

## Why it matters

Global institutions are the international layer of macro policy and a rich synoptic topic — linking exchange rates, the balance of payments, development, protectionism and government/market failure. They give you real-world, evaluative context (especially Egypt) for top-band answers.

keyTerms.definition: An institution promoting global financial stability and lending short-term to countries in crisis.
An institution providing long-term loans and grants to promote development and reduce poverty.
The body that promotes and regulates rules-based free trade and settles trade disputes.
Policy conditions (e.g. austerity, reform) attached to IMF/World Bank loans.
Loan conditions requiring market liberalisation, fiscal discipline and reform.
The 1944 conference that established the IMF and World Bank.
Policies to cut a government's budget deficit through lower spending and/or higher taxes.

diagrams: No specific diagram is required. Support answers with **AD/AS** (austerity shifting AD left), the **FOREX/exchange-rate** diagram (devaluation) and **current-account** analysis from Unit 9.
tips: Distinguish the **IMF (short-term stability & BoP crises)** from the **World Bank (long-term development)** — a common exam distinction.
Explain **conditionality/SAPs** and give a **balanced evaluation** (stability & confidence vs austerity, poverty and lost sovereignty).
Link IMF programmes to **exchange rates, the current account and inflation** from Unit 9 for synoptic marks.
Use the **Egyptian IMF programmes and EGP reforms** as a powerful, current application/evaluation example.
Mention modern shifts toward **institutions, governance and the SDGs** for up-to-date evaluation.
